chapters.title: When devotion to God meets the truth of who you are
chapters.text:
Garrard entered the world in what looked like a textbook American household. His dad, an honest and shrewd automobile dealer, had taken up the pulpit and wanted to steer the faithful toward heaven with sermons and prophecy. His mother, soft spoken and watchful, was the calm counterweight to her husband. With that backdrop of piety and quiet, their only boy was expected to inherit the same fire for the Lord. His parents pushed him into chapel outings and Scripture drills, and he filled his calendar with both. Then the floor dropped out. The day Garrard realized he was different, that his pulse quickened around other young men, his neat little world collapsed.

A few faith traditions treat same sex attraction as an unforgivable trespass, lumped in the same category as drinking or hard drug use. Followers of those traditions often endorse conversion programs, regimented attempts to rewrite what they label as defective wiring or moral failure. Their complaints are not limited to sexuality. They take aim at slices of mainstream culture, from the Harry Potter shelf to orchestral recordings, claiming such pastimes pull hearts away from the Lord and stain tender minds.

Where you live shapes what you believe, but what stirs inside you is what makes you, you.

Garrard was caught between the doctrine he had been handed and the person he was becoming, and the pressure was relentless. He whispered the same plea to God night after night, asking to be scrubbed clean and freed from the endless loop of doubt. Could a child deny his deepest cravings in service of a higher power, or did he have to walk away from his family and his small town life to claim his real self. The pages ahead trace the route Garrard Conley chose and the ripple it left across his years.

chapters.title: Always on the outside looking in
chapters.text:
Standing out in any way was Garrard deepest dread. He worked overtime to look ordinary, to keep anyone from suspecting the truth about his private yearnings, so he asked out Chloe, a bright and easygoing girl from his congregation. Her laugh could lift the coldest room, and Garrard was drawn to that warmth. For the first time, he felt something close to ease. Walking into Sunday service with a girlfriend won him nods of approval, and the two of them made an adorable pair in the school hallways. In truth, Chloe was more than a useful screen. She was a real ally, and the two of them shared dumb jokes, late night music, and card games for hours.

What started as a wide eyed teenage romance shifted the moment Chloe floated the idea of getting physical. Her gentle fingers tracing his jaw in the back seat froze him cold. What kind of excuse could put off such a critical step. Faith stepped in with a convenient out. They should hold off until the wedding night. It was the only reprieve Garrard could think of, and it did little to settle him, since he could see the longing in Chloe eyes and knew the moment was creeping closer. At the start, he had hoped his heart would catch up. By now, he was no longer sure.

Garrard worried that parading Chloe around was not enough proof of straightness. What if someone read through the act. What if the elders who dropped by his father office to pray started to size him up. His sexuality was not the only place he felt off. His opinions did not match the visitors either. Once, a respected church deacon asked Garrard what he thought of the war raging across the Middle East. The man insisted America should flatten the area with a single nuclear strike to end the bloodshed for good. The men around Garrard leaned in, waiting. Their stares pressed down on him. Should he speak honestly and admit he sympathized with Arabs, or should he toe the elder line and nod along with the path of force. The heat was unbearable, and his tongue refused to move. The deacon offered a soft, that is fine, and turned away.

It is a lonely thing to celebrate what makes you different inside a room full of people who all think the same way.

chapters.title: A friendship fades into the background
chapters.text:
The idea of sleeping with Chloe hung over Garrard like a homework assignment. He had convinced himself it was a duty he owed, a public demonstration of the straight life. They agreed on a movie night at her place, and the two of them understood without saying a word that the evening was a milestone. Every attempt to set a tender mood was sabotaged by Chloe little brother, who kept barging in with jokes and noise. To a point, his presence was a relief, because it pulled attention away from what was about to happen.

The plan was to crash in the basement, and Garrard intended to slip into Chloe room once the house was still. Restless inside his sleeping bag, he caught the sound of a voice, and the game console was on before he could think. The two boys dropped into a pixelated world and played for hours. While they were building a character, Chloe brother pointed out that his avatar looked gay. Their eyes locked, and the truth passed silently between them. They played until the first light crept in, and Garrard finally exhaled.

The failure to perform on demand pushed Garrard to walk away from Chloe. No matter how hard she tried, she could not understand the reason, and he dodged her calls and ducked her at church. The whole congregation buzzed about the sudden split, but Garrard stayed silent. To escape the weight of his real life, he sank into marathon gaming sessions, playing through the night. Cutting ties with Chloe also cut him off from her brother, the only person who had shared his secret.

Terror over being seen makes people shrink from their surroundings and seek a quiet corner.

Before Garrard left for university, word came that Chloe parents had uncovered the truth about their son orientation. Her mother, in pieces, wanted Garrard father to talk the boy out of his wicked thoughts. The preacher came home late, looking broken and quiet. There was something in his face Garrard could read, a quiet suspicion. His dad sensed the incident had something to do with his own boy and the storm inside him. Soon after, Garrard called his parents into the bathroom, lowered his PlayStation into the tub, and let the water run over it. It was his way of saying he was done hiding inside the game world. Whatever waited for him, he would meet it head on.

chapters.title: A bond that turns into a nightmare
chapters.text:
Garrard guilt over hurting Chloe tipped into a punishing need to shrink. Before his first semester, he shed fifty pounds and alarmed his parents with his new frame. Yet he liked the change. His body now matched the lean ideal splashed across magazine covers. He felt ready to begin a new chapter on campus, close enough to home to visit, far enough to stand on his own.

The first friend Garrard made on campus was David. They clicked the moment they met, sharing hobbies and a faith background. David worshipped at a Pentecostal fellowship, where the room erupted in song, tongues, and the occasional collapse on the floor. Some Missionary Baptists, including Garrard father, had no patience for that kind of show. They could not stomach such theatrical displays in the sanctuary. Even with the gap between them, Garrard agreed to visit a Pentecostal service to see the noise for himself and to grow closer to his new friend.

What Garrard did not see coming was the horror that came next. After the service, David assaulted him and admitted he had done the same to a thirteen year old child. The experience was devastating, but in his head, Garrard heaped the blame on himself. Why had he let David do that if they were evenly matched. Was it some kind of payback for his own fantasies and his attraction to men. In his private reckoning, he felt no better than a predator, and he carried that secret alone. The part about the younger child, though, was too heavy to keep quiet, and he told the campus pastor. To his shock, the pastor advised silence.

Shame has a way of drowning out the voice of self respect.

David was not finished. He dialed Garrard parents and revealed the truth about their son. It was a gut punch to the preacher and his wife. Garrard father went so far as to threaten to cut off tuition if he went public with his orientation. The careful mask Garrard had worn in front of his family finally fell away for good.

A quick note: Pentecostal worship leans on spiritual experience, while Missionary Baptists put their weight on careful Bible study and steady work.

chapters.title: The mission of Love in Action
chapters.text:
Garrard parents were convinced that reparative therapy at Love in Action was the only path forward. LIA was a faith based outfit that worked with so called atypical souls, including gay and lesbian individuals, cheating spouses, and educators who had been pushed out after rumors of their orientation spread. The leader of the outfit was a controversial voice. Early in his work, he had gone on record saying death was a kinder fate than living with the wrong sexuality. A few members took that message at face value and ended their own lives. After the wave of bad press, the leader grew more careful with his words, and his new counsel was simply to follow the Lord.

Your sexuality is woven into who you are, and no one gets the right to rewrite it or look down on it.

LIA staff were mostly former graduates of the program. To do the work, they had to wall themselves off from secular life. They could only chat with people approved by LIA, and they had no access to the web or other popular sources. LIA was a circle of people terrified of losing everything they had. They believed the wider world would brand them as outlaws the moment they embraced their real selves. Like the rest of the room, Garrard was ready to scrub his sins clean and earn his way back into the community.

In a small town cinema, you could almost never find a film that mentioned homosexuality in the open, and when one did, the story almost always ended with a character dying of AIDS.

Intake meant surrendering every personal item, phone and wallet included, and a careful look at the patient outfit. Anything that felt provocative, a feminine shirt on a man or a masculine cut on a woman, was placed in the FI, False Images, category. The same rule applied to anything edgy stored on a phone. Staff even ripped pages out of Garrard notebook because the stories he wrote had earned an FI label. Patients left with handbooks full of homework for the evenings. For Garrard, the program was set to run two weeks straight, nine to five every day.

chapters.title: Inside the LIA therapy rooms
chapters.text:
One tool the staff leaned on was the genogram. It looked like a regular family tree, but it also tracked a history of so called defects across generations. The letters next to a relative name pointed to a specific issue. A meant alcohol, D meant drugs, and G meant gambling. The staff believed there was a clear thread between the sins of grandparents and the so called abnormalities of the patient. Garrard caught on to what the diagrams were really about. Seeing flaws in the family tree was a strange comfort, because it gave him something outside himself to blame for the way he felt.

Every night at home, the people in the program did an MI, a Moral Inventory. They had to write about the deepest secrets of their lives, whether it was a fantasy or a same sex encounter. The next day, they had to read those entries out loud to the group. The counselors believed the practice could drain the soul of the sins a person had committed and leave them spotless. It also produced an ocean of shame, guilt, and humiliation.

We had to be ready to undergo extreme changes, walk away from people who hurt our growth, and leave behind the things that reminded us of the past.

LIA pushed people to disown their nature and the most precious pieces of who they were, in the belief that it would draw them closer to heaven. It made Garrard wonder why the Lord would ask for such a brutal offering. Why was He so harsh with His people. While running through the streets at night, Garrard decided he had to break free. He could not hear the Lord voice anymore. He started pulling away from LIA, because the pain was no longer worth the cost.

Relentless humiliation makes people obedient and less able to stand on their own two feet.

When the Lie Chair exercise came up, Garrard could not bring himself to play along. He was supposed to sit across from an empty chair, picture his father in it, and pour out every ounce of anger he had been carrying. But he loved his father. He could not turn on him or scream the worst things he could think of at the empty seat. That was the final straw, and a clean reason to walk out the door.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Garrard and his family have almost no photographs from 2004, the year his orientation became visible to everyone. It is as if they instinctively wanted to bury any trace of that dark stretch of inner war, pain, and doubt. Since then, Love in Action has changed in many ways. Former counselors have spoken out about how ineffective reparative therapy is and how it scars the people it claims to serve. Among those voices was the leader of the group, who had run the meetings Garrard sat through. He also admitted on national television that homosexuality is not something anyone can or should cure.

Reparative therapy has shattered the lives of countless people around the globe. Those individuals wanted to find peace in faith and feel the warmth of those around them. Instead, they found themselves trapped. The cruel words and degrading drills made them doubt their own existence and the truth of what they felt. The lucky ones escaped the program at an early stage and learned to live without shame. Some have not bounced back. They wrestle with conditions like bipolar disorder and PTSD, and they live under the weight of suicidal thoughts.

Garrard lost his tie to the divine. He tried to find his way back to faith by sampling different churches and studying other traditions, but the fire he felt as a child did not return. Still, Garrard is not without hope. He is waiting for the day he hears that voice again.

Try this

If this story speaks to you, watch the film adaptation of the book. The movie, also called Boy Erased, was directed by Joel Edgerton and stars Nicole Kidman and Russell Crowe.

Keep reading to the end.
